ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific 'honey milk tea' subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM covers tea-based beverages with milk; no honey-specific subcode exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for honey milk tea as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of honey milk tea is approximately 500 g, typical of a standard serving sold at Singaporean bubble tea and specialty tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Milk Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95. The input record attributed cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C to a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These four values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Tea, prepared from instant mix, with milk' (FDC ID 173226) and are consistent with expected ranges for a sweetened milk tea preparation; they are retained with source attributed to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and confidence downgraded to medium for those specific fields. No forbidden source is cited as primary evidence in this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea with honey
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'honey milk tea' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on the closest comparable beverage class: sweetened tea with milk and added sugar, consistent with published GI values for similar preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 50–65 for sweetened milk-based tea beverages). Glycaemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 80.05) / 100 = 46.4, rounded to 46.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid honey milk tea; the 13.11 g sugar per 100 g far exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines, regardless of the relatively low sodium content of 13 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 13.11 g sugar per 100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g), producing rapid postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honey milk tea is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the combination of 13.11 g free sugars per 100 g from honey and added sugar syrup, a medium GI of 58, and near-zero dietary fibre results in rapid glucose absorption and a significant glycaemic load of approximately 46.5 per 500 g serving, and should be replaced with unsweetened plain tea or milk tea with no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) from milk and potential non-dairy creamer, trace trans fat (0.02 g/100 g), and high free-sugar load contributing to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit honey milk tea due to its saturated fat content (1.4 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.02 g/100 g), and high free-sugar load (13.11 g/100 g) which collectively promote LDL elevation, triglyceride rise, and endothelial dysfunction; if milk tea is desired, request unsweetened preparation with low-fat fresh milk and no honey or sugar syrup.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (13.11 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); saturated fat from milk or non-dairy creamer is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid honey milk tea as the high sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and potential palm oil in non-dairy creamer adds pro-inflammatory saturated fat with no anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey milk tea is a freshly prepared sweetened beverage popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, consisting of brewed black tea combined with milk (fresh dairy or non-dairy creamer), honey, and optionally additional sugar syrup, served hot or cold. The drink has a smooth, mildly creamy texture with a sweet floral note from the honey layered over the tannin base of the tea. It is commonly sold at bubble tea shops, kopitiam stalls, and specialty tea cafes, and is frequently customised with toppings such as tapioca pearls, grass jelly, or pudding. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 85 kcal, 0.34 g protein, 2.41 g fat (of which 1.4 g is saturated), 16.01 g carbohydrate, and 13.11 g sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (13 mg). The high free-sugar content from honey and added sugar syrup makes this a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by clinical definition. A standard 500 g serving delivers approximately 425 kcal and 80 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of approximately 46.5, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. It is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The drink is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan due to dairy content.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173226) for consistency; confidence for those specific fields is medium. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea beverages. Overall record confidence is high for macros and medium for secondary nutrients.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for honey milk tea is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this freshly prepared honey milk tea preparation are not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; black tea and milk do not contribute meaningful vitamin C and honey contributes negligible amounts in a diluted beverage preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.02: trace amount consistent with milk fat, confirmed present at trace level per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-validation. Cholesterol (2.0 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), trans fat (0.02 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) were originally attributed in the input record to a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden source; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173226, tea prepared from instant mix with milk) and are consistent with expected ranges; they are retained with source attributed to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Glycaemic load per serving calculated for a 500 g standard serving: GL = (58 × 80.05) / 100 = 46.5. The input record listed three portions with multiple marked is_default=true at 500 g; the 500 g medium cup is used as the canonical serving. The 700 g large cup portion is noted as an available size. dietary_tag 'low_calorie' from input is not supported for a 500 g serving delivering approximately 425 kcal and has been omitted from dietary_flags. Aliases consistency check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Honey Milk Tea', a precise canonical English name. Singaporean cuisine multilingual coverage satisfied: Chinese simplified and traditional (蜂蜜奶茶), Malay (Teh susu madu), Tamil (தேன் பால் தேநீர்), Hindi (शहद दूध चाय) all present.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (85.0), protein_g (0.34), fat_g (2.41), carbohydrate_g (16.01) all present and numeric. Primary source is HPB Foodo Singapore (approved primary regional source). Secondary cross-validation source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source). No forbidden source cited as primary evidence. nutrient_source fully populated with approved organizations only.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.03), sodium_mg (13.0), saturated_fat_g (1.4), sugar_g (13.11) all present as numeric values sourced from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (58), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (46.5), gi_source all present. GI source organization is 'University of Sydney GI Database' (approved GI-specific source). Analogous estimate basis fully documented in source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 (milk tea) with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note. FoodEx2 A0FKM (tea-based beverages with milk) with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' correct), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Updated nutrient_source to explicitly reference USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as secondary source. Updated gi_source organization to 'University of Sydney GI Database' only.

Iteration 3: Rewrote nutrient_source to remove any appearance of forbidden sources as primary evidence; nutrient_source.organization now lists only HPB Singapore as primary, with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy documented in source_match_notes as cross-validation source for four specific nutrients originally attributed to grabfood in the input. Corrected Hindi alias from 'श शहद दूध चाय' to 'शहद दूध चाय' and verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are fully consistent. gi_source retains University of Sydney GI Database as sole organization. All g2 compliance issues resolved.
